stewart_read: Customer discloses a co-owner — her daughter — at 00:32. Rep says 'Okay' and moves immediately to utility company. No follow-up on the daughter: is she a co-decision-maker? Is she reachable for the appointment? The codex is explicit that a co-owner on title must be included in the appointment. Strategic read: rep may have planned to address this at button-up. Reactive read: rep missed the trigger entirely and never returned to it. Since no appointment was booked and no button-up occurred, the strategic read has no supporting evidence.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep planned to address daughter inclusion at button-up — but button-up never happened, so this cannot be confirmed.
coaching_implication: Manager should replay 00:32 with Bucky. The correct move per protocols.adult_child_decision_maker is to flag the daughter immediately: 'Is she involved in decisions like this? We'd want her on the call with us.' This is not a button-up item — it's a verify item that shapes the entire appointment structure.

quote: that tree on, like, the south side of your house, are you willing to trim it up a bit if we can offer something good?
stewart_read: Rep is working from satellite imagery and proactively checking south-side shading — the one genuinely strategic move on the call. The customer's response is a strong enthusiasm signal: 'There is no tree at this time. All the trees have been trimmed or removed.' Clean south-facing exposure with no shading is a high-value qualification signal. Rep says 'Okay. Sounds good' and moves on. The signal warranted a brief anchor: this home's roof profile is favorable, which is exactly what the closer needs to build a compelling design.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have noted the shading clearance internally and planned to reference it at pitch — but no pitch occurred, so this cannot be confirmed.
coaching_implication: Manager should highlight the satellite check as the right instinct, then show Bucky how to anchor the customer's answer: something like 'That's actually great news — south-facing exposure with no shading is exactly what makes a system perform well. That's going to make your design look really good.' One sentence. It costs nothing and raises the customer's investment in seeing the design.

stewart_read: Rep captures the bill number and moves on. The codex's bill_anchoring pattern is capture + acknowledge + flip. Rep does capture ('Yeah') but neither acknowledges the pain nor flips it. '$200 on a small house' is actually a useful data point — it tells the closer the usage profile is modest, which affects system sizing. But the emotional opportunity is also present: even a $200 bill is $2,400/year going to the utility. Rep left the flip on the table.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk Bucky through the capture+acknowledge+flip pattern using this exact exchange. The flip here isn't about a high bill — it's about redirecting $2,400/year. Note: do not fabricate a savings number. The coaching point is the emotional redirect, not a specific figure. The codex's numeric_discipline applies.

quote: Current reading, 7863. Previous reading, usage, yes, 1482.
stewart_read: Verbal readback was working. The customer navigated to the Dominion portal, found the usage screen, and was reading numbers aloud. This is the bill_collection.alternative_paths.elderly_low_tech_protocol in action — the customer self-selected into it. The coaching point is not 'pivot to verbal' (the codex's standard note for this protocol); it is 'you were already on verbal — stay there.' Rep instead pivoted to a photo request at 05:48, which the customer flagged as awkward ('It's on my monitor') and which ended in a FaceTime accident.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have wanted the monthly graph for system-sizing precision, which verbal readback of a single usage number doesn't fully provide. Strategic read: photo captures more data. Reactive read: rep defaulted to the standard photo protocol without recognizing verbal was already succeeding and that pushing photo on a customer at a desktop monitor was high-friction.
coaching_implication: Manager should replay 05:34–06:00 with Bucky. The question to ask: 'At the moment the customer was reading numbers to you, what were you thinking?' If Bucky wanted the monthly graph, the coaching is: 'Ask them to read each month's bar aloud — it takes 60 seconds and you get the same data without the photo friction.' The photo pivot cost the appointment.

quote: I just got a few questions to get started. Is that okay?
stewart_read: Rep skips the program pitch entirely and moves straight to verify. There is no curiosity created, no legitimacy reinforced beyond the intro line, and no emotional hook before the qualification checklist begins. The call runs as a data-collection exercise from the first 16 seconds. The customer complies, but compliance is not engagement.
coaching_implication: Manager should note that the program pitch beat (call_shape.required_beats order 3) was skipped. One sentence of curiosity creation before verify — 'We've been helping homeowners in Arlington offset their Dominion bills, and I want to see if your home qualifies' — changes the customer's frame from 'answering questions' to 'finding out if I qualify.' This is a setup issue, not a close issue.

quote: Is there anybody else on the title but you?
stewart_read: The verify section captures: address/homeowner (00:21), co-owner (00:26), utility company (00:35), average bill (00:42), roof type (00:52), credit (00:55). Missing: interest_in_solar — the codex's 'emotional anchor' and the single most important verify item — prior_designs, and roof age. The 6-of-8 required_completion_threshold was likely not met. Interest_in_solar is the item the codex says top setters spend 60+ seconds on; Bucky spent zero seconds on it.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk through the verify checklist with Bucky item by item. The missing interest_in_solar question is the highest-priority gap — it is the emotional foundation the entire pitch should rest on. Without it, the rep has no anchor for why this customer cares, which makes the closer's job harder. Suggested coaching question: 'What would you have done differently if you knew she was motivated by bill pain vs. environmental concern vs. a neighbor's recommendation?'

stewart_read: After ~90 seconds on hold, the customer returns and asks 'who are you?' — a small but real trust reset. Rep does not re-anchor: no name, no company, no re-statement of purpose. Rep says 'And then,' and moves directly into the tree question. The customer's confusion ('It's fine. Who are you?') signals the hold eroded the connection. A one-sentence re-anchor ('Still Bucky with Ion — appreciate your patience') would have reset the frame.
coaching_implication: Manager should flag this as a micro-skill: any hold longer than 60 seconds requires a re-anchor on return. The customer's 'who are you?' is not hostile — it's a genuine reset request. Bucky's non-response to it is a missed rapport repair, not a major failure, but it compounds the call's overall low-energy trajectory.

stewart_read: The call ends in technical disarray. Customer accidentally initiated a FaceTime call. Rep asks 'Hello?' and the transcript ends. No appointment booked, no callback time locked, no recovery attempt visible. The FaceTime accident is a proximate cause — the root cause is the rep's decision to push the photo path after verbal readback was already working at 05:34.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this as a teaching moment on recovery: even when a call goes sideways technically, the rep needs a recovery line ready — 'No worries, let me call you right back' or 'Let's skip the photo — you already gave me the numbers I need. Let me get you scheduled.' The absence of any recovery attempt here is the coaching gap, not the FaceTime accident itself.
